cases.title: The Deceptively Stable Child
cases.presentation: A 5-year-old struck by a car has a tender, bruised abdomen. He is quiet and pale with a fast heart rate, but his blood pressure reads 'normal' for his age.
cases.question: How should you interpret his normal blood pressure?
options: He is in compensated shock and can decompensate suddenly—treat aggressively and transport rapidly
Tachycardia in children is always benign
cases.explanation: Children maintain blood pressure until a large volume is lost, then crash. Marked tachycardia with pallor and cool skin signals compensated shock from likely intra-abdominal injury. Provide oxygen, obtain IV/IO access, give a weight-based fluid bolus per protocol, keep him warm, and transport rapidly to a pediatric trauma center.
cases.clinicalPearl: In children, a normal blood pressure with tachycardia is a warning, not reassurance—treat before hypotension appears.

cases.title: Suspicious Injuries
cases.presentation: You are called for a 9-month-old who is 'not acting right.' The caregiver says the infant rolled off a couch, but you note bruises of differing colors on the back and a spiral arm fracture. The story keeps changing.
cases.question: What should you do?
options: Confront and accuse the caregiver on scene
Provide appropriate medical care, document findings objectively, transport, and report suspected abuse per mandate
Ignore the injuries since the caregiver explained them
cases.explanation: Injuries inconsistent with the stated mechanism and developmental stage, bruises in different healing stages, a spiral fracture, and an inconsistent history are red flags for non-accidental trauma. Care for the child, document objectively without accusation, transport, and report per your mandatory-reporter obligations.
cases.clinicalPearl: Treat, document objectively, transport, and report—do not investigate or accuse. EMS providers are mandated reporters.

cases.title: Pediatric Airway Positioning
cases.presentation: An unresponsive toddler with a head injury is placed flat on a backboard. You notice his large head flexes his neck forward and his breathing becomes noisy and partially obstructed.
cases.question: What adjustment improves his airway and alignment?
options: Place additional padding under his head
Pad under his torso/shoulders to bring the airway and spine into neutral alignment
cases.explanation: A toddler's large occiput flexes the neck when supine on a flat board, obstructing the airway and malaligning the spine. Padding under the torso/shoulders restores a neutral 'sniffing' position, opening the airway while maintaining SMR.
cases.clinicalPearl: For young children, pad under the torso—not the head—to achieve neutral airway and spinal alignment.
